name: Scalability
description: The ability of a system to handle increased load by adding resources. Vertical scaling upgrades a single server; horizontal scaling adds more servers.
keyPoints: Vertical scaling is limited by hardware maximums and creates single point of failure
Horizontal scaling requires load balancing and stateless services
Horizontal scaling is preferred for production systems - scales indefinitely
Vertical scaling is simpler and requires no code changes
Cloud auto-scaling enables horizontal scaling based on traffic patterns
Stateless services are a prerequisite for effective horizontal scaling
whenToUse: Vertical for small apps and quick fixes. Horizontal for large-scale production systems requiring fault tolerance.
interviewQuestions: What is the difference between vertical and horizontal scaling?
When would you choose vertical over horizontal scaling?
How do you make an application horizontally scalable?
What challenges arise with horizontal scaling?

name: Load Balancing
description: Distributes incoming network traffic across multiple servers to ensure no single server becomes overwhelmed. Improves availability and throughput.
keyPoints: Layer 4 (Transport) routes based on IP/port without inspecting content
Layer 7 (Application) inspects HTTP headers for content-based routing
Algorithms: Round Robin, Least Connections, Weighted Round Robin, IP Hash
Load balancers provide health checks and automatic failover
Hardware (F5) vs Software (Nginx, HAProxy) vs Cloud (AWS ALB/NLB)
Can terminate SSL/TLS, reducing backend server overhead
whenToUse: Always needed with multiple application servers. Essential for high availability and zero-downtime deployments.
interviewQuestions: What are different load balancing algorithms and their trade-offs?
What is the difference between Layer 4 and Layer 7 load balancing?
How does a load balancer handle server failures?
What are sticky sessions and when are they needed?

name: Caching
description: Stores frequently accessed data in fast storage to reduce database load and improve response times. Redis for app-level caching, CDN for static content.
keyPoints: Cache-aside (lazy loading): App checks cache first, fetches from DB on miss
Write-through: Writes go to cache and DB simultaneously
Write-back: Writes go to cache first, async flush to DB
Eviction policies: LRU, LFU, FIFO, TTL-based
Cache stampede: Many requests hit DB when cache expires
CDN types: Push CDN vs Pull CDN
whenToUse: Redis for sessions, leaderboards, rate limiting. CDN for static assets, video streaming. DB caching for read-heavy workloads.
interviewQuestions: What are different caching strategies and their trade-offs?
How do you handle cache invalidation?
What is the cache stampede problem?
When would you use Redis vs Memcached?

name: SQL vs NoSQL
description: SQL databases are relational with ACID properties. NoSQL databases are non-relational with flexible schemas and horizontal scalability.
keyPoints: SQL: PostgreSQL, MySQL - complex queries, JOINs, transactions
NoSQL types: Key-Value (Redis), Document (MongoDB), Column (Cassandra), Graph (Neo4j)
SQL scales vertically; NoSQL scales horizontally by design
NoSQL offers eventual consistency, trading consistency for availability
SQL for financial systems, inventory; NoSQL for feeds, logs, analytics
CAP theorem influences database choice
whenToUse: SQL for banking, payments, complex transactions. NoSQL for social feeds, IoT, real-time analytics, high write throughput.
interviewQuestions: When would you choose SQL over NoSQL?
What are ACID properties?
Explain the CAP theorem
How do you handle schema migration in NoSQL?

name: CAP Theorem
description: A distributed system can guarantee at most two of three: Consistency, Availability, Partition Tolerance. Since partitions are unavoidable, the choice is between C and A.
keyPoints: CP systems: Sacrifice availability during partitions (PostgreSQL, MongoDB)
AP systems: Sacrifice consistency during partitions (Cassandra, DynamoDB)
CA systems are theoretical only in distributed contexts
Eventual consistency: Data will become consistent eventually
PACELC extends CAP: trade-off between latency and consistency even without partitions
whenToUse: CP for financial transactions, leader election. AP for social feeds, DNS, shopping carts.
interviewQuestions: Explain the CAP theorem with examples
What is the difference between strong and eventual consistency?
Can a system be CA? Why or why not?
What is the PACELC theorem?

name: API Design (REST, GraphQL)
description: REST uses HTTP methods with resource-based URLs. GraphQL lets clients request exactly the data they need in a single request.
keyPoints: REST: Stateless, cacheable, standard HTTP methods, resource-oriented URLs
GraphQL: Single endpoint, client specifies fields, supports nested queries
GraphQL solves over-fetching and under-fetching
gRPC: Binary protocol using Protocol Buffers for high-performance inter-service communication
API Gateway: Single entry point handling auth, rate limiting, routing
REST best practices: proper status codes, versioning, pagination
whenToUse: REST for CRUD apps, public APIs. GraphQL for complex data needs, mobile apps. gRPC for internal microservice communication.
interviewQuestions: What are principles of RESTful API design?
When would you choose GraphQL over REST?
What is the N+1 problem in GraphQL?
What is an API Gateway?

name: Consistent Hashing
description: Distributes data across nodes using a hash ring, minimizing redistribution when nodes are added or removed.
keyPoints: Hash ring maps both servers and keys to positions on a circular ring
Key assigned to first server found clockwise from its position
Adding/removing node only affects keys between that node and its neighbor
Virtual nodes: Each physical server maps to multiple positions for better load distribution
Used in Redis Cluster, Cassandra, DynamoDB, CDNs
whenToUse: Distributed caching, database partitioning, load balancing with session affinity, any system where minimizing data movement during scaling is critical.
interviewQuestions: What problem does consistent hashing solve?
What are virtual nodes and why are they needed?
How does consistent hashing handle node failures?
Compare consistent hashing with modulo-based hashing

name: Rate Limiting
description: Controls the number of requests a client can make within a given time window. Protects against abuse, DDoS, and system overload.
keyPoints: Fixed Window Counter: Simple but allows burst at window boundaries
Sliding Window Log: Accurate but memory-intensive
Token Bucket: Allows controlled bursts up to bucket size
Leaky Bucket: Processes at fixed rate, smooths traffic
Implementation uses Redis with atomic Lua scripts
Fail open if rate limiter goes down to maintain availability
whenToUse: Protecting public APIs, preventing resource monopolization, enforcing tier-based quotas, mitigating DDoS.
interviewQuestions: What are different rate limiting algorithms?
How would you implement a distributed rate limiter?
What happens when the rate limiter goes down?
How do you handle rate limiting across multiple servers?

name: WebSockets
description: Persistent, full-duplex communication channel between client and server over a single TCP connection. Both parties can send data at any time.
keyPoints: Initiated via HTTP upgrade handshake
Full-duplex: Both client and server send messages independently
Persistent connection eliminates HTTP overhead per message
Requires sticky sessions or connection-aware routing behind load balancers
Alternatives: SSE for server-to-client, Long Polling for compatibility
Challenge: Each WebSocket holds persistent connection; need horizontal scaling
whenToUse: Real-time chat, live notifications, multiplayer gaming, collaborative editing, financial tickers, live streaming chat.
interviewQuestions: What is the difference between WebSockets and HTTP?
How do WebSockets scale across multiple servers?
When would you use WebSockets vs SSE vs Long Polling?
What challenges do WebSockets introduce in load balancing?

name: Proxies (Forward/Reverse)
description: A proxy acts as intermediary between clients and servers. Forward proxy hides client identity; reverse proxy hides server identity.
keyPoints: Forward Proxy: Client-initiated, hides client IP, enables content filtering
Reverse Proxy: Server-side, handles SSL termination, load balancing, caching
Common reverse proxies: Nginx, HAProxy, Cloudflare
API Gateway is a specialized reverse proxy with auth, rate limiting
Reverse proxy enables zero-downtime deployments (blue-green, canary)
Always use reverse proxy in production to protect backend servers
whenToUse: Forward proxy for privacy, geo-bypassing. Reverse proxy for any production web app.
interviewQuestions: What is the difference between forward and reverse proxy?
Why is a reverse proxy important in system design?
How does SSL termination work at a reverse proxy?
How does an API Gateway differ from a reverse proxy?
